sentences.text: Nathan Lipschitz.
Nathan, N-A-T-A-- T-H-A-N.
Lipschitz, spelled L-I-P-S-C-H-I-T-Z.
Same, Nutu Lipschitz.
N-U-T-U.
Nusan.
Nusan Nutu.
Both names are Hebrew names.
No.
Doiv ?] is a middle name.
The birth date.
In English?
July, July 20, 1918.
In Hebrew, 16 days in Av.
[HEBREW].
My age is 77.
The city was a small town by the name of Wielopole.
W-I-E-L-O-P-O-L-E.
In Poland.
It was a small town of about 100 to 125 Jewish families, real small town.
The non-Jews were probably less except some villages around.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What is it that you would like me to say?
Like a little town with a ring, which is the-- a ring is the, the circle, and a few streets going out, side streets.
And that the whole pulse of, of the town was that town circle where the farmers used to come in with their wagons to sell wheat, or any other agricultural products.
Then going-- after getting the money, going and buying in the stores around.
There were all kinds of stores.
Whatever-- all kinds of stores.
I would say except one store was a non-Jew.
The rest, others were all Jewish people.
Anything you can think of, there was a store.
It's a small town but the shoe stores and the grocery stores and the piece good stores and clothing stores.
There was anything that is in the larger town.
And the Jewish community was pretty close.
There was one shul and one beis medrash.
And, of course, the Germans came in, they closed up the beis medrash, and they made, I think, a stable out of it for a while.
The shul was a tremendous, very big and very solid constructed with stone and bricks.
The thickness of the wall-- of the walls must've been at least a yard with these large windows.
This, the Germans did not destroy.
The beis medrash, they destroyed before they left.
This they did not destroy.
It was left over.
And then the Polish people made a movie out of it, movie house out of it.
And there's a very interesting and tragic story with, with that episode.
They had a movie in the early '50s, I think.
I read it in the Polish paper here, somebody pointed out to me and showed it to me.
They showed a children's movie and there were about 350 children or more, I don't remember exact, or more.
Closer to 400 children, about 30 adults that came with the children.
And all of a sudden a fire broke out.
They all got burned.
This construction, it was all brick and all stones.
Somehow everything got burned to the-- to the ground with the few hundred children and, and about 30 adults.
This was after the war already.
Now generally, the Jewish population was, I would say, let's divide it in about four categories as far as the economy goes.
At least a quarter of the population was very poor, quite poor.

As a matter of fact, now when I start thinking, you know, going back to the days and the night that I wouldn't think about it, especially going to sleep, not being able to sleep, and maybe this is the reason sometime for not being able to sleep.
There is still quite a number of people until this day, although it was-- I was 21 years old and my father was the rabbi and I knew everybody through and through.
And I used to go as a teenager, early teenager, used to go to collect money before Shabbos to help out the few families that were-- that were officially poor.
I still don't know to some of these people-- and this is not one or two, maybe 20 or so-- until this day, how they lived, how they existed.
One, one little-- I don't know if it's interesting to you that one person that I was pretty close with and here and there he came to me, maybe I can lend him a zloty or two.
And I did, and here and there he can give it back to me.
One day he says I can't give it back to you.
I don't know when.
And by the way, this is-- he told it to me how it go, but others, some may not even have that.
And this was at least a quarter of the town, of that, that little town.
There was another one who, as I told you, you used to go here and there to the villages, buy some products, and come into the city and sell it.
They were also poor, but they may have already for a piece of chicken for Shabbos.
And instead of eating bread with, with coffee for breakfast, they may even had once in awhile a piece of butter.
But very poor.
Then there was the so-called middle class who had some sort of businesses in town, or they had some son or daughter in America who helped them a little more.
So they were the middle class.

Now, the middle class, if you would look in an apartment there how they lived, it was always clean, but the kitchen with a room or those that have the small stores they had a kitchen, a little kitchen in the room in the back and the front was the store, and this was their whole living quarters.
And they were considered mostly the middle class.

As a matter of fact, some who were even better off and that could probably afford more, but they had it from their parents already and it went like this without, without any trying harder to get any better because he wasn't used to any better.
And there was a few who were considered rich.
[LAUGHS] You want to know, I'll tell you a little episode.
I don't know if it may interest you.
One day-- you know [?
Nittel, ?] what [?
Nittel ?] is?
You don't know.
There is one day that-- it's a sort of a strong [NON-ENGLISH], a strong traditional-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] traditional-- It's a custom.
--acceptance that one day we're not allowed practically to learn.
So we, this we-- we did.
The people-- play a little cards.
There was one man who was married in to the richest man in-- in town, so he was considered-- he was a very intelligent man.
He was considered-- he was a very rich man.
He invited us to come up to his house to play cards.
I just tell it to you because I just remind myself talking about it.
So we had played cards-- it was a Saturday night.
This is the only time that religious people played cards.
So in, in the middle, he got hungry.
He went over and he got himself some bread, put a little chicken fat on the bread, and he had some Shabbos leftover, a polke from the chicken, and he ate that.
Fine.
We finished.
We went down.
One guy says to us-- now he, he stayed in is house, right?
Says to us, look what rich people are.
Not only he ate bread with-- with chicken fat, but even a polke too with.
It's mind-boggling.
And he was saying that how, how rich he considered-- this is so rich that he can't afford both, both those things.
This is a little indication, a little to make you understand what the standard of living was.
It depends what you call large.
Well, we had two large cities in-- in one side and the other side.
One was Tarnow, which was, I would say, about, let's say in kilometers, let's say about 35, 40 kilometers.
And the other side of the town was Rzeszow.
In Yiddish it was called Rayshe.
And that was a little smaller town and this was a little closer, I would say about 35 kilometers.
And, and this was also pretty large town, industrial town.
People from the small town that they need merchandise to sell for their stores went here or in Tarnow.
And they went mostly with wagons-- wagons, horse and wagons.
But when I was a little kid already they started to go buses, so there was buses going in each town so it was a little easier.
Every morning this was the, the, the, let's call the [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] happenings in town. 6 o'clock in the morning, those that wanted to live a little excitement got up when the bus left.
The two buses left and said goodbye to people.
This was the big thing in the town like a rodeo, [LAUGHS] let's say.
This was the big thing.
Well, as I think I mentioned it before, that there was no officially address because just the name.
The mailman knew everybody.

But one day a guy came and he wanted to make probably a few zlotys so he made up little streets and, and, and came in and gave us-- we had to-- paid him a zloty, a little, and it said a street with a number.
It was [NON-ENGLISH] with number two.
It was built with wood, a wooden house.
There were a few rooms only.

We had large kitchen, a dining room, living room-- no, the kitchen was dining room living-- dining room and kitchen, and the other room was dining room, living room, and my father's [NON-ENGLISH] room, that as the rabbi.
And was all-- at night there was a little bedroom and there was another room.
There was the sukkah for Sukkos and also whatever was needed was used in other room, another large room.
That was the house.
And we were six children.
And this was the rabbi in town.
We were considered better than the-- than, than the middle class.
Only Yiddish.
Well, telephone-- there wasn't a post office.
If somebody wanted to call you from another town, it was called a visa.
So they will call to the post office and the post office send them a messenger with a little slip.
It was called-- I don't know what it's was called-- a visa.
And they let you know and what time you should come to the post office.
There was however-- and there was no electricity in town, but there was one big mill, mill and a what is called in Polish, Yiddish a tartak.
That means to cut wood, building materials, saws.
They had telephone.
This owner had a car, and he had electricity.
He wanted to sell electricity to the shtetl.
Nobody wanted it.
My father did want it and he was about to talk-- send over that he wanted to put it, so one [NON-ENGLISH] came and he says, Rabbi, how would it look?
The only one who would have electricity would be something that the people would be too jealous and would think who knows how much money you have.
So my father didn't do it.
Were you able to follow current events from your house?
Do you remember any discussions?
There was no radio in the whole Jewish community.
There was some goyim that had radios, but if there was something, a very, very big events, you heard it.
Now as far as papers, yes.
They Jew-- newspapers came in.
There was a paper came in.
It was daily papers.
It was also weekly papers.
This came in through the mail.
And also those that were a little more interested used to get the American Jewish papers.
Daily Jewish papers used to come.
Of course, with delays and then the paper went from one hand to the other, but it came, and it was there.
My mother's maiden name-- first you want her name?
Her Jewish name was Kazy Baile.
Her maiden name was also Lipschitz.
We're related.
Well, I would say third cousins or something.
Something like that, yeah.
No.
She was the rebbetzin, around the house with six children.
[LAUGHS] She didn't have free time to spend.
Everything was taken up by the house-- around the house, although we had a maid all the time.
And but there was one thing that now it's a forgotten thing, you know, of mending, mending socks, for instance.
It took a little while.
Or mending some-- some clothes.
There was here and there a little hole and doesn't mean you throw it away.
You make a patch, you know?
And this was already in our house which I told you that we were considered the upper-middle class.
This was a normal routine.
We had a maid already so that didn't mean that but that poor, but there still is the normal routine.
What do you mean you're going to throw away something that only needs a patch?
So and she also liked very much with, with charity.

She was-- this was in the summertime especially, there were Jewish-- Jewish poor people that went-- whole families in their wagon, a few families in a wagon and there were never-- the very lowest class.
Some people were afraid that they'll steal when they come into the house and they don't let them into the house.
Only when they came, they gave them the few cents and go.
And my mother, this is summer.
She was occupied almost everyday with this.
Couldn't take that and couldn't accept it.
So there was on the oven standing two pots, two black iron pots.
One was with coffee and one was with what we call [NON-ENGLISH].
That means a potato soup and everybody-- and our table was always spread bread with butter so everybody came in in the kitchen.
Here is a plate of soup, bread and butter, as much as they have desired.
And then this was almost every day-- except on Friday I don't remember-- this was every day in, in the summer.
Almost every day in the summer.
And this took a few hours doing this, quite a few hours during the day.

And then for Shabbos there was always some, some poor people that-- better-- better class of poor people who went around, they have to-- there was always Shabbos, almost always, I mean, especially in the summer.
Even the winter too.
These class went already didn't go where they went with-- with their wagon that they paid for it.
Usually it was the mail wagon that came.
Shabbos there were always-- there were a few people who took some of the poor people on Shabbos.
But the first thing was the rabbi's house, so she had to cook up a little more.
There was always one or two even three people with Shabbos table.
And this wasn't only us, but the rabbi was the first one and the most-- it always went to the rabbi's house.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
By the way, you're talking about the news, the important news.
Those people were the biggest news carriers, those poor people, [LAUGHS] because they went from town to town.
And I'm talking about the news in different communities and different towns.
You became a, a sort of a-- know a-- knowing a lot if you mingle around with people, always talk to them.
They love to talk.
I'm talking about those better class who came to the beis madresh.
And they-- from every community you had reports with names, you know, different news, you know?
This is not yet-- very interest-- is not yet, I read here and there some books, the people who wrote that shtetl, about the shtetl.
I haven't come across anybody to sort of write about this in a serious way in more details because these chapter of the poor people running from town to town is a very, very interesting.
In every town it was different.
It was very colorful even, but I haven't heard or seen anybody that give it the-- the-- the deserved chapters, the deserved way of writing it out.
My father's name was Yehuda Sindel Lipschitz.
How was it for me?
Growing up-- you've got a good question and it's a little bit touchy and therefore I won't [LAUGHS] touch on it too much, I wouldn't, because he was very, very strict.
Well, when I was fairly young-- fairly young, I mean really young.
So it was-- it was not bad because he came home, especially wintertime from shul it was about 4:30.
It was already after market, about quarter to 5:00.
He came home, sat down to learn.
At night he got up and took the tallis and tefillin, went to shul in the morning.
That whole winter night was sitting and learning.
So the next day, the next morning, he came back from shul.
He ate some breakfast.
Then sometime and say he laid down for awhile.
And then there was sometimes after he got up, there were always some rabbinical things to take care of, so he didn't have much time to me.
But I got bigger and the rabbinical [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] responsibilities and [? everything ?] was-- came larger, he didn't have that much time to sit the whole night and learn.
And then my mother got a little ill, so it was different.
He had somehow, he was more busy with the rabbinate and therefore there was sometimes a little time.
He was very strict with me, and I didn't have a lot of fun, no.
To him there was one thing-- if you learn, keep on learning then you'll write.
If you're not, no matter what you do is nothing.
So he was very strict to me.
He wasn't soft on any-- in any way or shape or form.
My mother was soft.
She always tried to, to soften up things, but he was very, very strict.
And now I'm thankful to him.
I realize how right he was because I-- I'm a father, a grandfather myself.
But as a teenager it was very, very tough, very miserable almost.
But he only knew one thing-- you got to sit and learn.
And if you just took off for a little while, you were already on the dog's list, [LAUGHS] so to speak.
But generally was a nice, warm life in the house and then the children all loved each other and we were a very happy family.
Sure ?], my grandparents.
A matter of fact, my grandfather that you see here [POINTS], the picture, he was still alive when all his children, grandchildren except me-- I was was already-- killed out.
And he, the Germans killed him in a-- in a ghetto in Bochnia.
He was-- he lived in another town.
He was rabbi before.
Before my father, he was rabbi, so it was his father, his grandfather rabbi in that same little town.
But then he moved away to another town.
The name was Brigel in Yiddish or Brzesko in Polish, Brzesko Slotwina it was called.
It's right near Tarnow-- between Tarnow and Krakow.
And he was a Hasidic rabbi there with a lot of Hasidim coming to him during the week to ask for, for guidance and for, for prayers.
And for the Saturday was a little more people, on the holidays was quite a lot of people.
And so he was a-- he was a different personality, all soft and goodness.
And when I came there this was my haven, this was my [LAUGHS] liberation.
I loved to stay there as much as I could.
And I could.
I stayed there for very often-- half a year, a year, a lot in that town.
Yitzchak Lipschitz.
His wife's name was Frieda Lipschitz.
Yeah, sure.
She was also-- she lived till Hitler gone and killed her.
The other grandparents I didn't know-- oh, except my mother's parents that is.
Her mother, they lived the last-- he was a rabbi also, also Hassidic rabbi, and he was a sort of a restless soul apparently.
He was for instance a rabbi in the little-- in a town Busk which was also a resort.
And one day he comes from shul.
And resort, of course, you know, there was a lot of resort style going around.
Once he came from shul, he went through another little street where he was usually going, he saw women going with bathing suits and this gave him such a shock.
He says I cannot-- will not be a rabbi in this town no more.
He came to his house, to the [NON-ENGLISH], and he said, pack up and we're going.
She was crying, how could you pack and go look for some other place?
Pack up and we're going.
Packed up and went out without knowing any place.
They went to Pinczow, where my grandmother was from.
They stayed there for awhile.
His grandfather's name was Leibish Lipschitz, or Aryeh Leib Lipschitz.
He was rabbi in a few towns, in [NON-ENGLISH] and Busk and [NON-ENGLISH] because he was a individual that could not bend to anything.
He had his principles and if it's not so he didn't care what, where, when.
If it's good for the family, oh-- his principles-- naturally principals in this case means the strict religious life.
His wife's name?
His wife's name was Hadassah.
And, and in the middle '20s around, he left to Israel.
He wanted to settle in Israel.
And my grandmother was supposed to come, you know, send the tickets, supposed to come to Israel.
He never could get the money together, and she was left there with her daughter that was never married because she was sick.
OK.
